data.title: Crocs Is Showing Clear Deterioration Signs, Moving To Hold
data.description:
Crocs, Inc. (NASDAQ:CROX) released Q4 2024 earnings, recovering to its post-Q3 '24 levels after a steep selloff. Back then, I recommended the stock at a price of $110. The rationale was a low valuation given the value of the Crocs brand and the strength of Crocs being offset by Hey Dude's weakness.

However, the Q4 '24 results worry me. I am concerned about the growing signs of deterioration, mainly in top-line deceleration, despite increased investment in marketing. While the stock remains cheap at 8–9x FY25 expected earnings, margin compression, and revenue deceleration warrant a more cautious stance. Operating leverage can make a low P/E look expensive in retrospect. I am moving the name to Hold.

4Q24 Results

Crocs clearly decelerating: The Crocs brand is still growing, but increasingly with more difficulty (and at lower profitability). The brand grew 5% in Q4 and 10% in FY24. But I'm concerned about several negatives.

North America flat in Q4: The region showed DTC growth but wholesale declines, which is generally a negative mix shift (DTC can be pushed more via performance marketing). Wholesale is a key indicator of future demand, and a decline suggests retailers are cautious. Management guided for flat North American revenue in FY25, with Q1 expected to decline in MSD. Google Trends for Crocs in the US are not good.

Crocs margins are contracting even at the brand level: The company is investing significantly in marketing, yet revenue growth is slowing. This dynamic is problematic: a strong brand should see higher marketing spend translate into accelerating sales, not decelerating trends. The company explicitly guided continued margin pressure in FY25 due to higher marketing investments.

For example, in FY24, Crocs (company) generated 200bps of gross margin expansion, but operating margin still fell by 200bps. This means more than 400bps of increase in SG&A, and only MSD growth guided for next year. For FY25, the company is expecting to lose another 100+bps of operating margin but expects sales in NA to remain muted.

In my last article, I considered that most of the operating margin loss occurred at the corporate and Hey Dude level, but 2H24 figures also show operating margin deterioration at the Crocs level (32.8% from 33.6% for Q4 YOY and 36.4% from 37.2% for Q3 YOY).

Again, deteriorating margins to drive moderate topline growth is a very bad sign for a brand because the trend of deteriorating margins will only get worse if revenues decline. The company/brand has crossed the limit of marketing effectiveness.

No signs of stabilization in HEYDUDE despite marketing: HEYDUDE showed a 4% revenue increase in Q4, marking its first positive growth quarter in five quarters. However, this was not enough to salvage the full year, which finished down 13% YOY.

Worse, Crocs guided HEYDUDE down high-single-digit in FY25, despite heavy marketing investments (including hiring actress Sydney Sweeney as a brand ambassador).

Valuation is Fair But Not an Opportunity

Crocs is guiding for FY25 EPS of $12–$13, implying a P/E of 8/9x at current prices. This valuation seems attractive for a company with MSD growth, strong brands (at least Crocs), and 24–25% operating margins.

However, the combination of slowing growth and declining margins makes this multiple less compelling, and even risky. Earnings can shift quickly when a company is spending heavily on marketing without generating incremental revenue. What looks like a low P/E today can turn expensive if margins continue to deteriorate.

Given these risks, I no longer see Crocs as an undervalued opportunity, but rather a stock that is fairly priced for its current challenges. For that reason, I move Crocs to a Hold.

data.title: Fubo: Like A Phoenix From The Ashes (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
A little over a year to the day from my last fuboTV (NYSE:FUBO) coverage for Seeking Alpha, we find a company in a drastically different position. When I wrote about FUBO in February 2024, my 'sell' rating was driven by the announcement of a sports streaming JV from Disney (DIS), Fox (FOX) and Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD). Even though the company pledged litigation against such a streaming product, my view at the time was that Venu Sports would ultimately be the nail in the coffin for FUBO. I held that view because fuboTV has long been marketed as sports-only streaming application - one that has been licensing the rights to redistribute content from other companies, including the three involved in Venu Sports.

Up until 2025, that call looked pretty darn good:

From the time I wrote "Good Night Sweet Prince" for FUBO last February through the end of the year, the stock had fallen by over 35%. Then the lifeline came. Like a phoenix from the ashes, FUBO is back having rallied from under $2 per share at the beginning of the year to over $5.

In early January, Disney and fuboTV agreed to a business combination that would see Fubo and Hulu+ LiveTV coming together under Fubo management. Subsequently, in what many found to be a surprising move given the settlement, plans to launch Venu Sports ended. Each of these developments are substantial wins for Fubo in my view and change my immediate outlook for the company.

In this update, we'll look at the specifics of the Disney deal, ongoing litigation concerns, and I'll get into Hulu+ Live TV metrics that FUBO shareholders should keep in mind.

Important Details of The Proposed Deal

The major points of emphasis from the proposed transaction are as follows:

  • Disney and Fubo would be combining the Hulu+ Live TV platform with the fuboTV platform through a new entity, or 'NewCo'
  • Upon approval, Disney would own 70% of Newco with current FUBO shareholders owning the remaining 30%
  • NewCo would trade publicly under the FUBO ticker
  • Hulu+ Live TV and fuboTV would continue as standalone services to provide consumers with better optionality
  • Fubo will receive $220 in cash in exchange for settling all litigation between the two companies. This cash settlement is not contingent on the closing of the NewCo as well as a $145 million loan commitment
  • Fubo's existing management team will oversee operations of NewCo and the board of directors will be largely appointed by Disney

For a company that I've truly felt was running out of time, this deal would be quite a lifeline for Fubo should it go through. Currently, NewCo would be at 6.2 million vMVPD subscribers between the two platforms and over $6 billion in annual revenue. Fubo is projecting $120 million in cost saving from synergies and efficiencies post-close.

To put it mildly, Fubo badly needed a catalyst given the company's debt load and $200+ million trailing twelve month cash burn. I've been very critical of the company's business model in the past. And while I still have plenty of concerns about that model, I actually think this is a pretty good deal for Fubo as it buys the company some time. The deal is expected to close in 12-18 months pending regulatory and shareholder approval. Which might not be as big of a slam dunk as it probably should be.

Monopoly Litigation Concerns?

Just as Venu Sports faced litigation challenges from Fubo directly, it appears as though the Disney/Fubo deal could also potentially face litigation challenges from additional MVPD parent companies and consumer advocacy groups. After having previously joined others in an amicus brief on Fubo's behalf following announced plans for Venu, the American Antitrust Institute sent a letter to the DOJ urging an investigation into the proposed business merger between Fubo and Disney. From that letter:

The settlement of the Fubo suit does not appear to address any of the concerns raised by the district court or those expressed by the Antitrust Division. It allows a joint venture to proceed that a federal district court judge has already found likely to be illegal. Worse still, under the guise of “strengthening” Fubo, the agreed terms instead eliminate Fubo as an independent competitor.

While plans for Venu have since been scrapped entirely, the AAI went on to encourage the blocking of both Venu and the Fubo/Disney combination. The American Economic Liberties Project also weighed in on the proposed deal with this:

Disney’s acquisition of Fubo as part of a settlement of that case represents a troubling escalation. By absorbing a direct competitor and critic, Disney is reinforcing its dominance in the sports streaming market and silencing opposition to its monopolistic practices. This move will leave consumers with fewer choices, higher prices, and less innovation in an already concentrated industry.

Frankly, I find some of these assertions to be borderline preposterous as they seem to disregard that Disney is a relatively small player in the eroding MVPD business from the standpoint of being a multi-channel distributor. Even if the proposed merger between Fubo and Disney were to manifest and consolidate down to one vMVPD platform (not even currently being proposed), the combined entity would still only be the sixth largest pay-TV provider given current subscriber numbers:

This is certainly not a MVPD business that I would categorize as monopolistic. To be sure, Disney is exposed to linear TV more broadly via carriage fees. Thus, Disney has quite a bit to lose purely as a content seller to MVPDs if the company's net revenue via DTC initiatives isn't positive - and this is far from a certainty. Which means to some degree, the interests of Disney and many of the companies in that chart are still aligned temporarily.

That said, if the claim is that Disney is monopolistic in the streaming space because it owns the rights to sports distribution through ABC and ESPN; that argument seemingly also fails in a world where Netflix (NFLX) and Prime Video (AMZN) exist - each of which have already entered the sports distribution rights market and have distributed NFL games. Comcast (CMCSA) owns the second largest MVPD and has a sports streaming footprint through Peacock that includes Sunday Night Football. Alphabet (GOOG) owns YouTube TV and holds the rights to NFL Sunday Ticket. Then there are CBS (PARA) and Fox - each of which hold NFL rights.

Meaning, no less than seven different public companies have distribution agreements to stream NFL games and only two of them were involved in Venu Sports in the first place. The NFL is admittedly just one sports league, but it's the most popular league in the United States and serves as an important illustration; mainly that the way sports programming is distributed is changing, there is very little MVPD companies can do about that long term, and the hand-wringing over Disney's sports streaming monopoly has arguably been overblown whether the Fubo deal closes or not. My gut instinct is that this Fubo/Disney deal will be approved by regulators and shareholders. And that brings us to the long term outlook for FUBO shareholders assuming NewCo happens.

Disney Might Be Dumping a Dead Man Walking

It would have seemingly been very easy for Disney to just settle litigation with Fubo and move on. The fact that management of Disney's vMVPD streaming product would be turned over to the Fubo team as part of the agreement is a really interesting element of this deal that I think is more significant than it might seem. My hunch is multi-channel linear distribution is a business that Disney simply might not want to be in any longer. And it would be hard to blame the company if that is indeed the case considering Hulu+ Live TV hasn't meaningfully grown subscribers in the last two years.

While the monthly ARPU for Hulu+ Live has grown 21% from about $82 four years ago to over $99 last quarter, extracting more revenue from a generally stagnant subscriber base is probably not a great long term strategy. We intuitively know there is a limit to the ARPU upside for Hulu+ Live because excessive cost increases to the end user is the very thing that triggered the cord-cutting movement over a decade ago.

Revenue (b$) Hulu+ Live SVOD Only
2021 $4,007 $6,021
2022 $4,479 $6,418
2023 $4,938 $6,431
2024 $5,243 $7,034
YoY 6.2% 9.4%
CAGR 9.4% 5.3%

Source: Disney filings, Author's Calculations

While Hulu SVOD ARPU has not really grown at all in the last four years, the subscriber base has grown by about 30% from 37.8 million to 49 million subs. Even with the lack of growth in ARPU, Hulu's SVOD revenue exhibited stronger growth in 2024 with a 9.4% YoY gain against a 6.2% gain for Hulu+ Live. To be sure, Hulu+ Live has a better 3-year CAGR, but the year over year growth rate has declined each year since 2022 in spite of ARPU growth. Essentially, Disney is generating more aggregate revenue from Hulu SVOD and for the first time in at least three years, SVOD revenue had a faster growth rate than the vMVPD service as well. Given Disney has Disney+, ESPN+, Hulu, and plans to launch ESPN as a standalone service later this year, migration from the vMVPD business makes quite a bit of sense.

Of course, Disney would own 70% of the combined Fubo/Hulu company and still be very much exposed to linear TV as a result. But just because Disney starts with 70% ownership of NewCo it doesn't necessarily mean it has to keep it all. Regardless, I think this is an indication that Disney is preparing for life after MVPDs. The company plans to launch ESPN direct-to-consumer later this year. With TNT losing the NBA to NBC, it could quickly become much easier for consumers to justify trimming pay-TV subscriptions entirely if sports fans can get the bulk of their programming from ESPN directly for $25 and 2-3 additional apps. To be sure, diehard sports viewers will likely stick with fuboTV. But NewCo would be far more reliant on Hulu+ Live TV for revenue and that might not be a good thing longer term.

Closing Summary

I'm going to upgrade FUBO shares from a 'sell' back to a 'hold' mainly on the cash infusion from Disney. Given Fubo's cash burn, this deal would be a tremendous lifeline to get the company into 2026. I'm not all that convinced this changes the long term trajectory of the pay-TV model and because of that I'm not interested in a long term position in FUBO shares. While the balance sheet boost is nice, long term exposure to Hulu+ Live TV is probably not as wonderful as it might seem.

I suspect there is a reason why Disney is willing to pass it off to Fubo and it may have something to do with subscriber growth stagnation coupled with decelerating revenue growth. If Disney's launch of ESPN DTC later this year is going to disrupt MVPD services, Hulu+ Live TV will almost certainly not be immune from that. The company's willingness to move away from its own vMVPD offering is probably a signal. One more thing; Fubo CEO David Gandler sold nearly $22.5 million FUBO shares above $5 per share during the month of January. You might call that a signal as well.

data.title: U.S. - Subdued Start To 2025
data.description:
By James Knightley

Retail sales dragged down by weather and LA fires

We thought a soft January retail sales report was likely given the cold weather and the Los Angeles fires, but it is worse than even our pessimistic forecasts. Headline sales were down 0.9% month-on-month in nominal terms (consensus -0.2%) while the control group that excludes the volatile autos, food service, building materials and gasoline and supposedly better tracks broader consumer trends fell 0.8% (consensus +0.3%). As these are nominal value changes and we know prices rose 0.5% MoM according to the CPI report, this implies very weak volume sales growth. It is the volume measure that feeds through into GDP growth. There were some slight upward revisions to the history, but even so the market is going to be disappointed with 10Y yields down 6bp on the back of this.

Auto sales fell 2.8% MoM, largely because of a big unit volume drop, which we already knew about, while furniture dropped 1.7%, electronics fell 0.7%, health spending fell 0.3%, clothing down 1.2%, sporting goods dropped 4.6% and internet sales fell 1.9%. Surprisingly, eating and drinking out actually rose 0.9% – we had expected this to drop because of very cold weather and there was a sense that the Los Angeles fires would also have a depressing effect, but somehow this was one of the very few sources of strength.

Could tariff worries also have played a role?

In trying to rationalise this outcome, we know that consumer confidence did fall quite a lot in January, largely on a sense that prices were going to rise on tariffs (big rise in price expectations and the responses showing it was going to be a worse buying environment for big ticket items – which are often imported). My sense has been that we could see consumer durable purchases actually be very strong in the early part of the year as people buy their foreign made fridge freezers and washer/dryers etc ahead of tariff-related price hikes, but maybe people are getting confused on the tariff story. Perhaps they think tariffs are happening immediately and are therefore not even considering a purchase. Alternatively, many housheolds may feel under pressure – the Philly Fed reported a record 11% of credit card holdings are only making the minimum monthly repayment and the NY Fed reported a pick-up in credit card delinquencies in the fourth quarter of 2024 – with the threat of tariffs pushing up their food and energy bills some households may be considering cutbacks on other items ahead of time.

We will need to wait until the February data to see if this is the start of a more cautious consumer trend, or indeed whether it was simply a weather-related pullback and we get a subsequent big gain.

Manufacturing output held back by the weather while utilities demand surged

Rounding out the week's US numbers, industrial production rose 0.5% MoM, above the 0.3% consensus prediction, but manufacturing output fell 0.1% rather than rise 0.1% as the market predicted. As with the retail sales report, weather-related factors probably weighed on the manufacturing sector and mining, which fell 1.2%, while the cold weather prompted a jump of 7.2% MoM in utility output as we tried to keep warm. Overall though, today's US retail sales and manufacturing numbers suggest a very subdued start to 2025.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: Exciting Technology At Personalis, But Profitability Eludes Them
data.description:
Today’s analysis is going to be a little bit different, in that we’re talking about a cancer-related company. Cancer is something that impacts almost everyone’s life at one time or another. I lost both of my parents to it, and I don’t know many people who haven’t lost at least someone close to them over the years.

I’m not saying that because I want to turn this into a pity party, but rather to make clear in advance that I intend to look at Personalis, Inc. (NASDAQ:PSNL) as objectively as possible as an investment without taking personal feelings into account. That doesn’t mean that I, and most everyone else, don’t have an emotional stake in companies like this being a success, but that’s beyond the scope of a financial analysis piece.

As you can see, Personalis’ stock has had some relative success in recent months, though it has come off the peak of its trading range. We’ll be looking at the mixed earnings report of Q3, the preliminary numbers provided for Q4, and what their prospects for growth look like going forward. Personalis is not a profitable company yet, so it’s very important for prospective investors to determine if the company has a path to profit, when that might be, and how much money they stand to make.

First, though, I’d like to introduce you to Personalis’ technology.

Personalis and Ultrasensitivity Testing

As important a company as Personalis hopes to become, they’re nowhere near a household name. Therefore, it seems important for us to look at what the company offers and how they do business.

Personalis offers cancer genomic tests and analytics. The focus of the company is on ultrasensitivity in the testing, being able to detect cancer long before it actually shows up in imagining, or many other tests. Early detection is huge for survival in many types of cancer, and if they can detect a microtumor before it even shows up on the scans, that’s a really good start.

The tests come mostly within two distinct offerings. NeXT Personal, a liquid biopsy test that has been commercialized with the help of Tempus AI (TEM), is particularly valuable in early detecting the recurrence of cancer in people with remission. ImmunoID NeXT, by contrast, is a tissue-based test that provides an advanced view of tumors, microtumors, and the environment they exist in, to allow doctors to plan the most effective treatments for them.

With increased awareness of the importance of early detection, the testing market is growing rapidly, and Personalis’ offerings seem to fit nicely into that market. They also offer some exome and genome sequencing in their business. The hope is that Personalis will increase its market share in that growing market, and by that, become a serious, profitable company.

Cash and Equivalents

$76 million

Total Current Assets

$170 million

Total Assets

$240 million

Total Current Liabilities

$36 million

Total Shareholder Equity

$167 million

(source: most recent 10-Q from SEC)

For a growth company, the balance sheet isn’t wildly important, but I think it’s important to point out that they have a decent cash position, a strong current ratio of 4.72, and no real debt yet. Debt can crush a lot of growth companies, and while Personalis may have to take some on between here and profitability, they’ve avoided it so far. That’s an encouraging sign.

Right now, Personalis is trading at a price/book ratio of 1.94, which is somewhat lower than the sector median of 2.21. Again, that’s not hugely important in the grand scheme of things, but it still seems worth mentioning at least.

Growing, But Profitability Is a Fair Way Off

A nice balance sheet and exciting products is certainly a start, but the elephant in the room is that Personalis is not yet profitable. That’s a big problem, but hopefully a temporary one, as they continue to grow and pare away those operating losses.

(source: most recent 10-Q from SEC)

As you can see, this is still a very early-stage company in some respects. Personalis is cutting down those losses, but it may be years before they reach the break-even stage. As a small company, the analyst estimates offered are also pretty sparse, and not updated as much as would be ideal.

In Q3, we saw mixed results compared to the estimates, missing GAAP earnings per share by 29¢ but beating revenue estimates by $4.38 million. Q3 results were overall fairly positive on the growth front, with the biopharma business growing 96% year over year. Tempus AI also exercised some warrants and increased their investments in Personalis (more on that later). There are still some struggles with unreimbursed test costs, though, which are likely to continue to be a drag on gross margins.

With regard to the Q4 estimate, we have another revenue beat coming. Last month, Personalis gave us preliminary revenue numbers of $16.8 million. That’s down a bit year-over-year, but it’s significantly higher than the $15.48 million estimate we had beforehand. We don’t have a good handle on the likely EPS for the quarter yet, but the previous estimate expected a 28¢ loss.

Longer term, again, we don’t have a ton of estimates overall because Personalis is still such a small company. But the general trend is revenues growing each year and loss falling, with the figures we have predicting the first profit coming in 2029. That’s well in the future, but if they keep coming in with revenue ahead of those estimates, it’s also possible they’ll reach break-even slightly sooner.

It seems like the better-than-estimated revenues they've been coming in with should eventually compound into them hitting break-even sooner than 2029, of course, but a lot of that depends on them improving the gross margins. I think the technology should merit them continuing to meet or beat revenue estimates, though, and the EPS estimates seem attainable. It seems like they're on the right path here.

Tempus AI Partnership and Merck’s Investments are Positive

Personalis is small, but some bigger companies are starting to take notice. In late 2023, Tempus AI announced a “strategic collaboration” with them, helping Personalis to commercialize NeXT Personal Dx. This deal also included Tempus AI promising to help them fund clinical evidence development, to prove that these tests are worthwhile. Tempus AI was given 9.2 million warrants in the process, and they’ve exercised some of them already, injecting yet more cash into Personalis going forward.

In December, Merck (MRK) also announced that they had acquired 14 million shares of Personalis through a private placement. They paid $3.56 per share, and that suggests that even with likely Q4 losses, that next balance sheet should have even more cash on hand on the books. This gives Merck a 16.5% stake in Personalis.

Dilution is something of a valid concern here, but as a small company Personalis has been having some difficulty in getting insurance companies to reimburse them for tests. Ties to much bigger companies like Merck and Tempus AI should not only give them valuable capital, but more credibility on the national stage. They’re a company to take seriously, which is likely why the news of a Merck investment led Personalis stock to surge that day.

Exciting Possibilities, But It May Be Premature

I’m hesitant to invest in any company whose first profit is several years out. That said, there’s no denying that Personalis’ technology has some very exciting possibilities. The real question is how they will navigate this environment as they try to grow into a mature, profitable testing company.

There’s a lot of competition out there in the cancer testing space, and other companies will doubtless try to come up with early detection technology even better than what Personalis offers. Though the company seems to be right at the fore today, there’s no guarantee that they’ll remain there.

The bigger risk, I feel, is the problems with getting reimbursement from the tests they perform. Personalis is doing a solid job in increasing the number of tests they process, but they have to get insurance companies to actually pay them for those tests if they want to be a business. In the most recent conference call transcript, they conceded they’re still facing headwinds in getting reimbursed, and that this is continuing to be a drag on efforts to improve their gross margins.

One can only hope the continued success of their clinical trials in showing the efficacy of their testing will not only convince more doctors to use these tests, but will make it harder and harder for insurance companies to justify denying the claims. That’s always an uphill battle in this market, but the early success of their tests seems promising.

Watchlist Worthy, But Be Cautious

I really like what Personalis is doing, and have high hopes for them getting to profitability down the line. That said, I feel like we need more clarity right now before pulling the trigger. I’m rating them a hold, with a bias toward buy.

The revenue beats are quite encouraging, yet in Q3 they beat revenue but missed on earnings, and that’s somewhat concerning. The lack of really good analyst estimates, though not uncommon for such a small-cap company, makes it much harder to understand where this company is headed, and more importantly, when.

Even more important, it's hard to definitively evaluate what Personalis is worth, and what they're likely to be worth in the future. That's always a challenge with smaller cap growth stocks, and the unknown unknowns in the US healthcare system only compound on that. I feel like the potential for growth and the willingness for Merck to pay $3.56 per share gives us a good base-line for the least the stock is likely worth. Is it worth more? Time will ultimately tell, and with technology like they have, it easily could be.

Investors should keep a close eye on their continued growth, but especially how the gross margins are progressing. Personalis needs to find ways to get their margins higher, and that likely means getting reimbursement more easily and quickly on the tests they do. If they’re successful in that, I’d definitely upgrade them to a buy.

data.title: Stock Market Sleeps On Stereotaxis MAGiC CE Mark
data.description:
Stereotaxis Finally Gets MAGiC CE Mark Approval

When I first invested in Stereotaxis (NYSE:STXS) in April 2021, I expected MAGiC to get approval in Europe in late 2022 and 2 years after that in America. That was a mistake as the details regarding manufacturing, regulatory submissions, and approvals took much longer than expected. Finally, on January 27th, 2025 it was approved in Europe. The approval label is for the delivery of “local lesions in cardiac tissue for the treatment of cardiac arrhythmias.” This is a nice broad label that will allow robotics to be used how they have been historically in EP (electrophysiology).

The most frustrating aspect of this process was the approval was never in doubt. MAGiC worked well in its human feasibility study as I previously documented. Now the business model can work properly. When Stereotaxis’ robots are used to do ablations with MAGiC, it will get an additional $3k per procedure. These incremental sales have +80% gross margins.

There’s still a little bit more work to do because 13 of its 35 accounts in Europe require tenders. I spoke with CEO David Fischel about this process. He told me each country has its own timeline and conditions. It’s mostly paperwork and waiting. Each country having its own timeline explains why he has given a range of 6-12 months for tender acceptances. I expect them to be done around August of this year. As a reminder, there are five robots in Finland, four in France, two in Sweden, one in Denmark, and one in Norway. Clearly, we want Finland and France to accept the tenders first. For the first few months of commercialization, GenesisX orders will be limited to the European countries that don’t require a tender for MAGiC.

MAGiC FDA Approval & Usage To Drive Stock Starting August-September

I’m disappointed with the stock’s lack of movement in reaction to this pivotal milestone. In response to this, I concluded the best chance of a rally is in the August to September timeframe. That’s because in August the firm will report its first full quarter of procedures with MAGiC (Q2). While I expect MAGiC’s adoption to go smoothly and production to scale quickly, the switching process will probably take at least 1 month. This means Q1 will only show partial results. Stereotaxis also should be getting at least some of its tenders around August. At this point, investors will be able to quickly project how fast these hospitals will adopt MAGiC (should be slightly faster than the first 22).

Furthermore, I believe Stereotaxis will get FDA approval of MAGiC in September. This will further compound it recurring revenue momentum. According to the clinical trial website, the European trial for FDA approval will now reach its primary completion in February. That’s around what I expected because the trial was expanded to more hospitals post approval. If there are any types of patients the trial needs to add, the firm can collect the data from additional hospitals. Had Stereotaxis gotten the CE Mark a few months earlier, it would have completed this data collection earlier. It took so long to get the CE Mark, that the trial had already collected most of the data it needed by the time MAGiC got it. It’s possible no additional data is needed from these new hospitals.

Besides finalizing data collection, Stereotaxis needs the FDA to complete a manufacturing audit of OSYPKA’s facility. I’m working under the assumption that the manufacturing audit is scheduled for May and will be complete by the end of the month. Then, if it takes 90 days for a decision, we should get an approval by September.

GenesisX First Order

I expect GenesisX to be approved in America sometime between the end of February and early March. I came to that conclusion based on 2 factors. Firstly, it took about 8 months to get Genesis FDA approved from 2019-2020 (it got two 510(k) approvals). Secondly, Stereotaxis probably submitted its response to the FDA’s questions in late November or early December. Late February or early March are 90 days from then. It doesn’t matter exactly when GenesisX is approved because MAGiC probably won’t be approved until late in the summer or early autumn (GenesisX needs MAGiC to work). Even if MAGiC is approved a couple months earlier than I expect, it wouldn’t matter if it took until the spring for the robot to be approved. However, investors love to see the innovation plan de-risked. I certainly want GenesisX approved as quickly as possible.

On the Q3 call, David signaled there was an initial order of GenesisX which would be announced quickly after MAGiC’s CE Mark. This was announced 3 days later on January 30th. I saw some investors discount this news because it had already been previewed. However, it’s important to avoid being too close to the story that you miss the forest for the trees. This first installation will be a major accomplishment because GenesisX makes magnetic robotics accessible for the first time. Given that the firm mentioned in the presser that it is advancing towards its commercial launch, I believe supply chain issues need to be worked through.

It’s pivotal that this first GenesisX installation creates an effective practice. Hospitals are always hesitant to try a new technology. The success of this lab will beget more orders. GenesisX’s start will be slower than Genesis’ because Genesis was a replacement product that was sold to existing robotics labs. While the start will be slower, the compounding momentum will be much greater with GenesisX because of its ease of installation. Orders will snowball.

Furthermore, Stereotaxis will submit its MAGiC Sweep robotic mapping catheter to regulators in the coming weeks. Upon approval, this will not only give Stereotaxis an additional $2k in high margin revenue per procedure, it will also further the benefits of robotics. MAGiC Sweep will bring the reach, stability, and flexibility that make robotic magnetic procedures so safe to the mapping process before the ablation.

China Update: Magbot Approval

On December 9th, right after my last article came out, Stereotaxis got NMPA approval in China for the Magbot robotic catheter. Specifically, Magbot was approved for the ablation of drug-resistant persistent atrial fibrillation, atrioventricular nodal reentrant tachycardia, and atrioventricular reentrant tachycardia. That’s a nice broad label.

This robotic catheter works with Niobe and Genesis (GenesisX probably not coming to China for a while). It was made in collaboration with Microport EP/EverPace. The design is similar to MAGiC. It works with MicroPort EP’s Columbus 3D EP Mapping System. Stereotaxis will get a royalty on this catheter. MAGiC will hopefully be approved in China later this year. MAGiC works with Abbott’s EnSite X system and J&J’s CARTO system (works better with Ensite X). Magbot and the Columbus 3D mapping system financially incentivize MicroPort EP to use its salesforce to push Genesis.

I believe Stereotaxis will get a better royalty on Magbot than it got from J&J’s catheter which is still being used in the US (royalty ended year end 2023). Stereotaxis had gotten its own robotic catheter approved about a couple decades ago, but went with J&J’s safer design. It makes sense Stereotaxis only got a $300 royalty (10%) because it was J&J’s catheter working on its robots. In this case, Magbot is Stereotaxis’ design working with its robots and MicroPort EP’s mapping system. I think a 50/50 revenue split is a reasonable estimate.

I’m excited to see the revenues from Magbot and the increase in Genesis orders in China this year. Obviously, Genesis’ replacement cycle and greenfield orders haven’t boosted Stereotaxis’ sales enough in America and Europe. However, I believe China’s push into robotics and its laxer construction constraints will allow for a bump in orders/installations. The MicroPort EP collaboration was a smart move because Stereotaxis doesn’t have the financial strength nor the expertise to jump into China. It already has its hands full in Europe and America.

Soft Timing Update On Stereotaxis’ New Products

Stereotaxis’ innovation plan consists of quite a few products beyond the MAGiC catheter and GenesisX. Each quarterly update gives us an estimate of when each product will be submitted to regulators, approved, and commercialized. There is always a nearly 4-month gap in between the Q3 and Q4 calls which creates a bit of uncertainty for investors. We got a small glimpse into Stereotaxis’ potential progress when the firm posted on social media that it is showing off GenesisX at the EHRA (European Heart Rhythm Association) Congress in Vienna which is from March 30th to April 1st. I believe this will be the unofficial commercialization launch of GenesisX. The firm should be able to deliver GenesisX robots at a reasonable pace by then. It’s notable that GenesisX is so portable that it can be brought to conferences and test driven. This should build interest.

Within this social media post, the firm said it would be doing test drives of the EMAGIN catheter and demos of Synchrony and SynX. EMAGIN is the branding for the families of robotic guidecatheters and guidewires. To me, this implies Stereotaxis is on schedule to submit the robotic guidecatheter to regulators in Q1. Stereotaxis’ acquisition of APT was mostly about acquiring the human capital to fully develop this device and the MAGiC Sweep robotic mapping catheter. William Hart, who was the VP of manufacturing and operations at APT, became the Senior Director of Operations at Stereotaxis last August.

As a reminder, the 5 indications Stereotaxis is going after with the robotic guidecatheters and guidewires are ischemic & hemorrhagic stroke, tumor embolization, peripheral artery disease, coronary angioplasty, and abdominal aortic aneurysm. I spoke with David about these various indications because I’ve seen incorrect information in sell side reports on each device’s indication potential. Both the robotic guidewires and guidecatheters can be used in the peripheral/coronary/neuro fields. Each device will be used in a different way. It’s not like each one is for a specific field. For example, in the neurovascular field, robotic guidewires can be used to get into aneurysms and robotic guidecatheters can be used to help get up the carotid.

I believe Stereotaxis will self-register Synchrony since it’s the computer system for doing the procedures (quick approval). SynX is just an app that doesn’t need regulatory approval. Synchrony is much easier to install than Odyssey Vision. SynX is the updated version of Odyssey Cinema which allows live procedures to be shared which is great for collaboration.

Timeline Updates

These are my updated timeline expectations based on the latest information I have. I removed the milestones that have been achieved. This table helps us organize each timeline to make sure things are on schedule without confusion. The robotic guidewire regulatory submission is the most uncertain because it has been delayed for so long. However, if it is delayed another few months, that won’t impact the stock.

Investors mostly want to see sales momentum from MAGiC in 2025 and evidence of GenesisX orders coming in. GenesisX will be the primary driver of growth in 2026 on a run rate basis. These GenesisX greenfield installments will drive an increase in recurring sales from MAGiC and MAGiC Sweep. 2027’s growth will be partially driven by expansions into future indications via the robotic guidewires and guidecatheters. I believe it will take time to go from approvals to mainstream adoption in these new indications. I expect a lot of testing in the next 2 years with studies showing positive outcomes for patients.

Q4 Earnings Call Expectations

Stereotaxis switched to reporting results in the morning. It will report before the open on Monday, March 3rd. The call is now at 8:30AM. This is much better than the calls that would start at 10AM (prior to 2024) because they went on during the trading session.

Stereotaxis is expected to report a 7-cent loss and $6.43 million in sales in Q4. The only potential upside scenario is if APT manual mapping catheter sales are strong, but I don’t expect them to be a big needle mover. I believe APT will hit $5 million in annual sales in 2025. Since, it won’t reach that run rate yet in Q4 2024, I expect it to produce about $1 million in sales.

Q4 will be the last uninteresting quarter. Starting in Q1, the firm will have some sales from MAGiC and Magbot. Management will probably start giving detailed guidance in 2026 once procedure volume hits a predictable level. As for now, analysts will use David’s tone and qualitative answers on the Q4 call to adjust the consensus estimate for $7.67 million in Q1 sales and a 4-cent loss.

Let’s go over the information I want provided on the March call. Some won’t be given, but these points will help you think about the company’s future. I want to know when the first GenesisX will be installed. I’m expecting it to occur in the spring. Future orders will be installed much quicker. I want a general understanding of the interest hospitals are showing in GenesisX in Europe now that MAGiC is approved. If any more orders are announced, it would be a huge positive, but I think it’s too early for that. Once Stereotaxis can book its first GenesisX sale, we can see its price. Right now, we only know it costs more than Genesis which is priced at $1.5 million.

I expect to see 1-3 new Genesis replacement orders in Europe and America. I want the Genesis backlog to decrease via installations. I would also like to hear about how Magbot’s usage is going in China. I’m hoping for 5 Genesis orders in China in 2025. I want to hear qualitative comments on APT mapping catheter usage. I want to hear about early MAGiC commercial usage. Stereotaxis needs to start giving procedure data now that it more significantly financially benefits from procedures and usage is in its control (no more supply chain issues with J&J catheter in Europe). Ideally, procedures per robot will rise due to improved clinical outcomes and the end of the J&J catheter shortage.

I would like to hear commentary on when the manufacturing audit will be done and when MAGiC will be FDA approved. On the last call, David hinted it will be approved in Q2 or Q3. It’s now 4 months later. I don’t need an exact month, but I’d like a more specific timeline that management is more confident in.

I want to know if/when the robotic guidecathers and robotic mapping catheter were/will be submitted to regulators. I’d like a hard timeline on when the robotic guidewire will be submitted. I’m hoping for this summer. I would like details on the MAGiC tender process. I’m hoping all the tenders have been submitted since the CE Mark was achieved about 5 weeks prior to the call. David probably won’t have a timeline on when they will be accepted yet. Finally, I want an update on the PFA programs they are working on. I’m hoping for at least one regulatory submission in Q2 or Q3.

Risk Factor: Cash Position

Stereotaxis is set to end 2024 with $12 million in cash and no debt. Investors have some concerns with its ability to hit breakeven without raising money. Management has no interest in issuing equity at the current low valuation. It’s possible the firm issues debt it can pay off in 2026 when it has strong profitability. Any form of clarity on this situation will be bullish for the stock. I’m sure it will come up on the March call.

Personally, I see it as a non-issue because any funding raise will be a small short-term bridge to profitability. A private placement that raises $10-$15 million would be viewed positively by investors. We haven’t seen Stereotaxis do major a dilutive financing round since the recapitalization in 2016 in which DAFNA Capital invested (David became the CEO shortly after). It certainly won’t do one now that probability is within reach (following MAGiC tenders and FDA approval).

APT’s $5 million in sales in 2025 will be margin/cash flow accretive since catheters have +80% gross margins. China Magbot catheter sales will give Stereotaxis additional high margin royalties in 2025. APT mapping catheter sales + Magbot royalties + some MAGiC sales should limit the cash burn in Q1. It should be in the $2-$2.5 million range.

If David were to guide for breakeven in Q2, it would help the stock. However, that might be too optimistic. It’s safer to suggest breakeven in Q4 with the FDA approval of MAGiC and the tenders achieved (plus, MAGiC Sweep sales). On the positive side, the quarterly burn rate will be $1-$2 million in Q2 and Q3. This isn’t a situation where the firm is burning a ton of money up until it hits FCF breakeven.

Stereotaxis isn’t hiring as many employees to sell MAGiC in Europe as I originally expected. It seems like Stereotaxis will keep the headcount relatively stable initially in Europe. David hinted the US requires more of a workforce increase to sell MAGiC. Stereotaxis should be highly profitable once MAGiC and MAGiC Sweep are approved everywhere and used in each procedure. Once gross profits increase sharply, I’m sure management will have no problem reinvesting the profits back into the business, growing headcount. I’m excited to see what the company can achieve once it has a sustainable model that provides cash for reinvestments.

data.title: Market Chaos Ignites Gold's Surge - Are You In?
data.description:
In January, gold miners outperformed as investor interest spiked. With low correlation to most assets, gold and miners offer strong diversification, making them an attractive choice today.

Got Gold?

If the answer is yes, you are one of the lucky winners so far in 2025. If the answer is no, take January as another great example of why you should want some gold exposure in your portfolio. A perfect storm of events - DeepSeek’s (DEEPSEEK) release, a tech stock sell-off, an unpredictable U.S. administration issuing a wave of executive orders, escalating tariffs and trade tensions and rising inflation concerns - shook global financial markets.

Historically, gold has served as the ultimate safe haven, as a hedge against market uncertainty and volatility, geopolitical risk and inflation, and as a place to hide when there is a heightened level of risk and fear. Yet, investor sentiment toward gold has been exceptionally weak for many years. However, in January, the market's focus shifted to many of the risks that have long supported gold. Defending its status as the ultimate market hedge and as an effective diversifier, gold performed exactly as history would predict - surging to a new all-time high of $2,798.41 per ounce on January 31, marking a monthly gain of $173.91 per ounce, or 6.63%.

Gold Rush 2.0: Western Investors Fuel the Comeback

Gold has had an impressive start to the year, potentially signaling the long-awaited return of Western investors. As the urgency to diversify portfolios grows, interest in gold is accelerating, evidenced by a 0.70% increase in gold bullion-backed ETF holdings. Gold is quite literally making its way west—to New York, to be exact. Trump’s tariff threats have ignited a physical gold rush, with investors and traders scrambling to secure bullion before potential price hikes. This has disrupted markets, widened price spreads between London and New York and drained London’s reserves, further tightening supply.

Typically, gold trades in contango (i.e., longer-dated futures contracts are priced higher than near-term contracts or the current spot price). However, it is now experiencing instances of backwardation, where the spot price exceeds futures prices. Gold’s 1-month lease rates jumped to as high as 4% recently, reflecting the crunch. Not bad for a non-yielding asset!

The Power Players Behind Gold’s Record-Breaking Surge

While market tightness puts upward pressure on gold prices, the real drivers of its strength are central banks and investors. The World Gold Council 2024 Gold Demand Trends report estimates total gold demand reached record levels last year, both in tonnage (4,974 tonnes) and USD value ($382 billion).

For the third consecutive year, central banks were net purchasers of more than 1,000 tonnes of gold - more than twice their average annual purchases from 2010 to 2021. By the end of Q3 2024, they had added 712 tonnes, leading many to believe they would fall short of the 1,000-tonne mark. However, they picked up the pace significantly in Q4 purchasing 333 tonnes, finishing just 6 tonnes below the 2023 total. This sustained central bank buying remains a strong pillar of demand, with the trend expected to continue in the long term.

Investment demand rose 25%, but this increase was the result of a slowdown in ETF outflows rather than fresh inflows. While India and China saw significant increases in physical gold demand, global bar and coin demand remained flat year-over-year. In other words, investment demand increased because outflows from the gold bullion backed ETFs slowed down significantly (-6.8 tonnes in 2024 vs -244.2 tonnes in 2023), rather than due to increased buying. A slowdown of outflows in 2024, followed by inflows into the global gold bullion ETFs in early 2025, could signal renewed Western investor interest in gold and the potential for higher prices ahead.

2024 Gold Demand Trends

Gold Miners Shine: A Breakout Start to 2025

The most exciting gold news in January was the strong performance of gold equities. As investor interest in gold grows, gold miners are delivering the expected outperformance relative to the metal. The NYSE Arca Gold Miners Index (GDMNTR)1 rose 14.91%, while the small/mid-cap MVIS Global Juniors Gold Miners Index (MVGDXJTR)2 gained 13.69% during the month.

Earnings season for the sector kicks off in mid-February. This is a crucial reporting period, as companies will release full-year 2024 results and provide 2025 guidance. Markets will focus on operating cost guidance, assessing margin expansion and free cash flow generation. Companies that meet or exceed 2024 targets and 2025 expectations may be rewarded, while those that miss could see their share prices decline.

Project updates will also be key, with investors closely watching timelines for permitting and production, as well as any capital cost revisions. Additionally, announcements on dividends and share buyback programs will be in focus.

For gold equities to be revalued from their historically low levels, high gold prices and renewed investor interest must be supported by strong performance from gold miners. The low correlation of gold and gold equities with most asset classes also enhances their diversification benefits, making them an attractive option for investors today.

Low Correlation of Gold and Gold Equities with Most Asset Classes, December 2004 - December 2024

data.title: International Paper: Working To Grow Sustainable Packaging With Strategic Investments
data.description:
International Paper Company (NYSE:IP), a global sustainable packaging solutions firm, is up 60.18% (YoY) after announcing its Q4 2024 and FY 2024 earnings results. The company also recently acquired British Packaging company DS Smith, a move expected to strengthen IP's global footprint. Despite the 2.3% (QoQ) revenue decline in Q4 2024, I will discuss why I am raising my stock rating to hold in light of the new synergies expected from its strategic acquisitions.

Financial Background

International Paper's annual revenue declined 1.57% (YoY) in FY 2024 to $18.619 billion, but its gross profit increased 0.85% (YoY) to $5.332 billion in 2024. Additionally, IP's net income surged 84.44% (YoY) in 2024 to $552 million from $302 million realized in 2023. The company is conducting various cost-cutting measures to increase profits, including the closure of some containerboard mills and other production facilities by April 2025 in a bid to also grow its investment footprint. More than 800,000 tons of containerboards will be removed from the market, a move I believe will raise the price. There was an 8.7% (QoQ) decline in IP's sales volume of containerboards in Q4 2024 that had also declined 7.4% since Q3 2024.

Despite its robust growth, IP has never shied away from conducting strategic shifts in its business portfolio. International Paper spun off its paper printing subsidiary, Sylvamo Corporation (SLVM) back in 2021 so that it could strengthen its packaging business and grow production. It has been a good run for Sylvamo, where its annual revenue has increased 33.4% (from 2021 to 2024) while its net income is up 33.04% (from 2021 to 2024). I believe Sylvamo’s success is attributable to its technological diversification despite the push for a paperless digital revolution. On its part, International Paper’s revenue has declined 3.8% (from 2021 to 2024) while net income is down 31.5% (from 2021 to 2024). IP's acquisition of DS Smith could not come at a better time.

IP acquires DS Smith to capture the market

The global sustainable packaging market is set to reach $305.19 billion in 2025. It is forecast to reach $552.45 billion by 2033, growing at a CAGR of 7.70% (from 2025 to 2033). IP has had the need to broaden its packaging business and readjust its portfolio. Corrugated packaging accounted for about 56% of IP's industrial packaging sales volume in the 12 months of 2024 despite its 6.54% (YoY) decline, indicating its resilient performance. However, it has been essential for IP to expand to the EMEA region since its sales are concentrated in the US.

IP closed its acquisition of DS Smith in an all-share combination valued at £5.8 billion ($7.2 billion, agreed in April 2024) after outbidding rivals Mondi and Suzano. Regulatory reviews by the European Commission caused the delay of the acquisition after its announcement in April 2024. In the combined business, IP Shareholders have 66.1% stake (347.4 million shares outstanding) while DS Smith shareholders have about 33.9% (178.1 million shares)- or a third ownership of the merger. This deal appealed more to DS Smith shareholders, despite Mondi and Suzanne's willingness to pay higher amounts. Even interesting is that IP is now trading in the London Stock Exchange (LSE) under the ticker symbol (IPC).

Each ordinary share of DS Smith is being exchanged with IP’s 0.1285 with a total of 178,126,631 shares being issued - meaning a larger stake for IP shareholders. The transaction value was about $9.9 billion as of January 31, 2025, when the deal was completed. This value is 37.5% higher than the original $7.2 billion agreed value back in April 2024. IP has a market cap of $29.34 billion against a total of 347.4 million shares outstanding. On the LSE, IPC’s total outstanding IP shares in the wake of this acquisition are 525,548,887 shares (of common stock with a par value of $1.00 each). This also means that DS Smith shareholders will receive the same dividend rights as IP shareholders. IP’s annual forward dividend yield stands at 3.32% distributed at a rate of $1.85 annually.

We are looking at IP’s robust expansion into the European and North American packaging market with this acquisition. About 90% of the merger’s revenue is also expected to emanate from sustainable fiber-based packaging, which is IP’s specialty. I believe IP has filled the void left by Sylvamo’s exit with this new entity. What's more, is that the synergies to be created have been forecast at $514 million. IP’s CEO Andrew Silvernail, while making the announcement, stated,

The combination of International Paper and DS Smith will create the world's leading sustainable packaging company, with a differentiated geographic footprint and an unparalleled suite of sustainable packaging products and services, we will accelerate growth, improve profitability, and serve our customers even better."

To add color, IP’s FY 2024 sales stood at $18.6 billion and an adjusted EBITDA of $2.0 billion while DS Smith’s revenue in the year ending April 30, 2024, was £6.822 billion ($8.5 billion) and an adjusted operating profit of £701 million ($872.1 million). This business combination is expected to raise IP’s revenue into 2025. In its FY 2024 earnings release, IP gave an EBITDA target of $4 billion that excluded DS Smith indicating massive cost improvement into 2025. Further, more than 90% of IP’s revenue comes from North America, while the remaining~ 10% is from EMEA.

The introduction of DS Smith will serve to grow sales in the EMEA region and strengthen the North American market.

Future earnings to consider for IP

In the last 4 quarters of 2024, IP's quarterly EPS has exceeded consensus estimates 2 times. Further, market analysis shows that the global sustainable packaging market is set to grow at a CAGR of 7.70% from 2025 to 2033. I believe, IP's annual revenue may also grow (and exceed the) CAGR of 7.70% in light of its global expansion with the acquisition of DS Smith by 2033.

As mentioned earlier, IP will also earn synergies (pre-tax) of at least $514 million after the merger with DS Smith. I expect it to hit this run rate by 2029 (annually) since it will get access to more than 30 countries operated by DS Smith. IP will revamp its global supply chain, and we are looking at the injection of operational and cost synergies with a target of $1.2 billion in FY 2025. Annual sales of the merger (based on the FY 2024 results) stand at about $27.1 billion, which is a 46% (YoY) growth in IP’s revenue heading into 2025.

Valuation and Risks

IP’s forward price-to-earnings ratio stands at 22.35X against the industry average of 19.86X (a difference of 12.50%). It shows the stock is slightly overvalued, and we may see some downside into 2025. However, this stock shows a different picture when contrasted with SLVM. Sylvamo has a market cap of $3.12 billion (about 9.4X lower than that of IP) and is trading 1.26X higher than IP. The forward price-to-earnings ratio of SLVM is 10.77X against the industry average of 19.86X (indicating a difference of -45.76%).

I would also like to consider Crown Holdings (CCK), a producer of consumer packages with a market cap of $10.35 billion. It has a forward PE ratio of 14.82X against IP's 22.35X. CCK is undervalued as compared to IP and trades 1.59X higher than IP.

However, the dynamics may change in 2025 with the entry of DS Smith into IP's operational matrix. By growing net income in 2025 through cost-cutting measures and revenue progression, IP will raise its EPS into 2025, thereby reducing its PE ratio.

Poor cost management remains a significant issue with IP. These costs brought down IP’s net income to $557 million, down from a revenue of $18.6 billion. Additionally, I believe the $1.2 billion cost reduction indicated in its Q4 2024 earnings release is still low, considering it will be implemented with the entry of DS Smith. I do understand that most expenses are attributable to its capital-intensive programs that include manufacturing and energy costs. I hope the price change of its linerboard and corrugating medium expected to take effect from January 1, 2025, is already in motion to help grow its revenues in H1 2025.

Bottom Line

I have rated International Paper as a hold, with the expectation that it will manage its costs after the acquisition of DS Smith and effect a product price-change to raise revenue in 2025. The acquisition of DS Smith is strategic and will help unlock additional shareholder value for IP through market expansion. The company is also closing down facilities to improve performance and lower costs in the long term. IP will hold its next earnings date on April 30, 2025, and I expect to review the performance of the new business combination.

data.title: Consider Shares Of Contango Ore: Poised For Higher Profitability Even With Worse-Than-Expected AISC
data.description:
A Hold Recommendation For Shares Of Contango Ore, Inc.

This analysis suggests a hold recommendation on NYSE-listed shares of Contango Ore, Inc. (NYSE:CTGO), an Alaska-based company engaged in gold exploration and production at Manh Choh, a high-grade gold project on lands of the Alaska Native Village of Tetlin. Contango has an interest in this project through a joint venture with Kinross Gold Corporation (KGC) (K:CA).

The share price has fallen sharply recently due to the company's disappointing estimates on its AISC/oz, but shares could fall even further, as we will explain later. Nevertheless, this stock is well positioned to rebound significantly as the growth prospects for gold prices are bright and Manh Choh has finally started producing the yellow metal in the third quarter of 2024. However, we advise waiting for the formation of even lower share prices than currently.

Let's start with the gold price.

The Gold Price Outlook

The price of gold has risen by almost 44% over the past 12 months, as evidenced by the increase in the troy ounce trading via the Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR). At the time of writing, the security was valued by the market at $2,917.26/troy ounce.

The price of gold is being driven by the Federal Reserve's one percent interest rate cut in 2024, as well as expectations that the US central bank will continue to cut rates later in the year. Lower interest rates increase the attractiveness of the yellow metal in competition with US Treasuries.

The yellow metal is also used as a safe haven as investors use it to protect the value of their investments from headwinds. Headwinds are plentiful at the moment, triggered by the geopolitical risk fueled by the conflict in Ukraine, the crisis in the Middle East and other tensions between countries around the world. Tensions are further exacerbated by the tariff war between the US and other North American countries, such as Canada and Mexico, and the US also engages in a tariff war against China and threats to increase tariffs on imports from the EU as well.

Due to the factors that have a positive impact on demand and sentiment towards gold, relevant experts predict even higher prices per ounce. As mentioned earlier, gold has broken several records in its endless rise, but based on analysts' gold price forecasts, the saturation point is far from being felt in the market.

According to Seeking Alpha:

UBS lifted its gold price target to $3,000/oz over the next 12 months, […]“The global gold rush continues, with the yellow metal again outshining other asset classes year-to-date as escalating trade tensions and rising geopolitical uncertainties draw investors toward gold as a portfolio diversifier,” UBS analysts said. The increased target comes as gold demand reached a record high of 4,974 metric tons in 2024, according to the World Gold Council, which also indicated that central banks' net buying exceeded 1,000 metric tons for the third straight year.

According to Seeking Alpha:

“Goldman Sachs analysts said gold remains its "highest conviction trading recommendation across commodities, driven by structural (Central Bank buying) and cyclical (ETF buying) factors," reiterating a $3,000/oz price forecast for Q2 2026.”

Experts at Trading Economics (see the “Forecast” section on this page of the Trading Economics website) have released average gold price forecasts indicating that the toy ounce will be at $2,939.54/ounce in 12 months

With this outlook for gold prices, investors may want to be exposed to the expected rise in the troy ounce. However, since investing in the physical precious metal directly is not affordable for everyone as it involves a financial commitment that only large investors or banks can leverage, the alternative for smaller investors is to invest in gold stocks listed in the US stock markets.

About Contango Ore, Inc. in Alaska. Its Correlation With Gold Prices

With this article, we shed light on the NYSE-listed shares of Contango Ore, Inc., under the CTGO symbol, as this gold stock has a pronounced positive correlation with the gold price changes after the pouring of the first gold bar in early July 2024.

Contango Ore, Inc. is a Fairbanks, Alaska-based gold producer with a 30% interest in the Peak Gold Joint Venture, which operates the high-grade Manh Choh gold mine on land owned by the Alaska Native Village of Tetlin. Kinross Gold Corporation holds the remaining 70% of the Peak Gold joint venture. Kinross Gold operates the mine and transports the high-grade gold ore from Manh Choh to the processing plant, the Kinross Alaska Mill in Fort Knox, 380 kilometers away.

The Manh Choh mine is expected to produce 225,000 ounces per year of gold equivalent (which also includes silver ounces) over 4.5 years of operation or approximately 1 million equivalent ounces in total. Contango production will be approximately 67,500 gold equivalent ounces per year or a total of 303,750 ounces over 4.5 years of operation.

The correlation between Contango stock price and gold price is shown by a yellow curve in the lower part of the following chart. The correlation is mostly positive as the yellow curve has been above zero for most of the last 12 months. A positive correlation means that when gold prices are higher, Contango shares are most likely trying to reach higher levels as well, which is valuable information, we believe, as gold prices are expected to continue their upward trend.

The correlation recently reversed and turned negative as, while gold continued to rise, Contango shares fell to a four-year low in trading on Friday (November 29, 2024), “after guiding for 25% higher costs at Alaska gold mine.” Seeking Alpha reports.

In our view, Contango Ore's share price will resume its uptrend in line with the price of gold. Before we explain why market demand for Contango Ore's shares will pick up again and trigger a price rally, we will use some current trend indicators to illustrate the first of two components that are paving the way for a strong price rally: the currently very low share price.

The Share Price Is Clearly Low And Very Close To Oversold Levels. Lower Levels Cannot Be Ruled Out At This Time. We Signal To Wait For Now

At the time of writing, Contango Ore shares listed on the NYSE under the symbol CTGO were trading at $10.01 apiece, giving it a market capitalization of $122.41 million.

Shares are trading below the MA Ribbon and very close to the lower boundary of the 52-week range of $9.65/share to $25.32/share.

The 14-day Relative Strength Indicator of 38.36x suggests that shares are very close to oversold levels, which can be considered as a base or support below which shares will not face much pressure but a counter-upside momentum for recovery will instead form.

With inflation data coming in hotter than expected on February 13, markets suspect the Fed will opt for just a 25-basis point rate cut rather than the previously expected two 25-basis point cuts this year. And the rate cut may not come into effect until autumn 2025. A growing belief that the Fed will remain “tight” on rates, which does not bode well for zero-yielding gold, will also negatively impact Contango Ore shares.

Therefore, we recommend a “Hold” for shares of Contango Ore for now.

Contango Ore Will Reverse The Trend and Re-Establish the Positive Correlation with the Rising Gold Price

We expect robust demand for Contango Ore shares.

The positive catalyst will be the profitability of Contango Ore's business, i.e., earnings or cash flow. Since profitability is a key factor for US-listed stocks, the principle is that an improvement in Contango's earnings or cash flow due to the sale of its gold equivalent production along with rising gold prices will regain the market's attention and Contango Ore's shares will ultimately recover well. That is the expectation.

We have already provided insights and forecasts on the topic of gold prices at the beginning of the article. Let's take a look at Contango Ore's business operations and how these, combined with expected rising gold prices, could affect the company's profitability.

Higher Costs Than Originally Expected Are No Reason To Worry

In November, Rick Van Nieuwenhuyse, President and CEO of Contango Ore, Inc., commented on the temporary AISC impact of new weight restrictions on freight at Manh Choh. He said:

“Overall, we are very pleased with the ramp up of Manh Choh production, expecting to produce 38,500 ounces of gold on Contango's account for 2024 and 60,000 ounces of gold for 2025. While we are disappointed with these new weight restrictions impacting Manh Choh trucking operations, which has an impact on AISC, the Manh Choh Project is expected to be very profitable over its current four to five year mine life, generating significant free cash flow to Contango.”

Compared to the US$1,116/oz cited in Manh Choh's 2023 technical report, life-of-mine (“LoM”) All-In-Sustaining Costs (“AISC/oz”) are expected to be higher at US$1,400/oz gold sold and are expected to be US$1,625/oz gold sold in 2025.

It is not possible to go into detail about the causes of higher AISC per ounce compared to the technical document unless these higher cost factors have already occurred in Q3 2024, that is, they have been experienced as part of reality, which is one thing, while a project on a piece of paper or a presentation on a slide is another. Since this assumption is reasonable, we believe that despite the higher-than-expected AISC (which may already be strongly on track to reach $1,625/oz in 2025), Contango Ore managed to hit strong profitability as early as the third quarter of 2024 (subsequent paragraph). The rising price per ounce of the yellow metal was very helpful.

The company achieved an average blended gold price of $2,252 per ounce of gold sold, as 14,825 ounces were sold via hedge contracts at $2,020 per ounce (net) and 12,850 ounces were sold at an average spot price of $2,521/ounce higher than the average market spot price of approx. $2,476.23/ounce in the third quarter of 2024.

Contango Ore Business: Profit and Cash Flow Performance

As a result, net loss has improved dramatically from a net loss of $13.2 million (or -$1.47 per share) in the third quarter of 2023 to a net loss of $9.7 million (-$0.81 per share) in the third quarter of 2024.

The net loss in the third quarter of 2024 is impacted by the charge of an "unrealized" loss on mark-to-market derivative contracts resulting from the comparison between a forward gold price and the contractually agreed hedging price. When the net income in the third quarter of 2024 is adjusted for this non-cash expense of $22.9 million, the result is the green for $9.7 million.

In addition, Contango Ore states that the main reason for the dramatic improvement in net income is that

“the Company's 30% owned, Peak Gold JV, commencing production at Manh Choh resulting in income from its equity investment in the amount of $28.5 M compared to a $5.6 M loss in Q3-2023.”

And with gold prices expected to be well above the $2,500/oz that Contango Ore assumed for its estimate of $50 million in cash flows from the Peak Gold JV in 2025, the stock is well positioned to improve its financial position. Contango can continue the efforts already made in the third quarter of 2024, aggressively deleveraging and continuing its exploration programs at Lucky Shot and Johnson.

These are all developments that the market can only welcome.

There were cash inflows from operating activities of $10.6 million during 9M of 2024 (as of the September 2024 quarter) compared to cash outflows from operating activities of $7.7 million during 9M of 2023 (as of the September 2023 quarter). The company had to draw down credit facilities of $30 million and raise cash proceeds from equity offerings of $15.5 million. Combined with cash inflows from operating activities of $10.6 million (for a total of $56.1 million), there were more than sufficient funds to cover investing activities, which were a total of $31.9 million during 9M of 2024 compared to $38.9 million during 9M of 2023.

Possibly Lower Demand For Credit Facilities Or Equity Issuance Promises A Good Climate For The Stock Price

With the Manh Choh project now producing gold and sales occurring at a significantly higher gold price per ounce, funds from credit lines and equity issuance are likely to be increasingly replaced by cash flow from operations. A more favorable year-on-year trend was already evident as draw-downs on the credit facility decreased from $17.7 million for 9M of 2023 to $15.5 million for 9M of 2024.

Regarding equity issuance, a lower share price undoubtedly impacted the lower amount raised through equity issuance but weighed on the number of shares outstanding (“S.O.”) that needed to be offered in greater quantity to raise sufficient funds: equity issuance decreased from $34.1 million in the first nine months of 2023 to $15.5 million in the first nine months of 2024, but S.O. growth was 2.3 million new shares in the first nine months of 2023 versus 3.2 million new shares in the first nine months of 2024. Improved profitability leads to the possibility of an increase in the share price, and with a higher share price comes a reduced risk of share dilution if the company still wants to raise money through equity issuance. Basically, the markets don't like the idea of ​​a number of common shares that might go up in the public market because a higher count of S.O.s means less earnings per share, which ultimately leads to a deterioration in the market sentiment towards the stock. This is the opposite of what is desired in terms of market sentiment when the company instead uses the funds to buy back its own shares from the public.

A Stronger Financial Condition Will Benefit Further Exploration In Alaska

As of September 30, 2024, Contango Ore had a total of $36.2 million in cash and ST investments on its balance sheet, burdened by $73.9 million of debt. As previously mentioned, the debt is merely a drawn-down credit facility and in the third quarter of 2024, encouraged by improved profitability backed by the first quarter of gold production in Alaska as well as rising gold prices, the company was also able to repay debts of $2 million.

Contango is well positioned to finance its portfolio of gold projects in Alaska, including Lucky Shot, a high-grade underground gold mining project located approximately 180 kilometres north of Anchorage, Alaska, and Johnson Tract, a gold-silver project located approximately 200 kilometres southwest of Anchorage.

Lucky Shot hosts 226,963 tonnes of indicated resources at an average gold grade of 14.5 g/t (grams of gold per tonne of mineral) containing 105,620 ounces of gold and 82,058 tonnes of inferred resources at an average gold grade of 9.5 g/t containing 25,110 ounces of gold.

Johnson Tract hosts 3.49 million tonnes of indicated resources at an average grade of 5.33 grams of gold per tonne, containing 598,000 ounces of gold. The deposit also hosts 673,000 ounces of silver, 400.8 million pounds of zinc, 43.1 million pounds of copper and 51.5 million pounds of lead.

Conclusion

Gold prices are rising, driven by lower interest rates and geopolitical risks that bolster its safe haven qualities. Investors may want to consider Contango Ore, Inc., an Alaska-based company engaged in gold exploration and production at Manh Choh, a high-grade gold project on the land of the Alaska Native Village of Tetlin. Contango has an interest in this project through a joint venture with Kinross Gold Corporation (KGC) (K:CA). Rather than investing directly in physical gold, which is not affordable for everyone, a position in Contango Ore shares is a cheaper and more practical alternative. The company’s profitability is benefiting from rising gold prices and production from the Manh Choh mine in Alaska following the first gold pour in early July 2024. The shares dipped as the company lifted its costs and adopted a gloomier cost outlook than previously published in the technical report. The market didn’t like it, but even at a higher AISC/oz, the likelihood of rising profitability is well protected by the robust prospects for the price of gold and 65% of the sales volume is hedged at a price much higher than increased costs per ounce. The company is well positioned to improve its financials and find new momentum to continue exploration in Alaska and take production beyond 4.5 years. In the short term, some issues could still affect the share price, so investors may want to hold out for a while.
